era_context: The Late Bronze Age is in advanced collapse. The Hittite Empire fell c. 1180 BCE, just two years before this year. Egypt under Ramesses III is facing mounting pressure from Sea Peoples invasions. Mycenaean Greece is in terminal decline. The Levantine city-states are fragmenting. In China, the Shang dynasty is in its final decades. This is a period of systemic crisis across Eurasia: climate disruption (possibly linked to volcanic activity), migration, warfare, and the breakdown of long-distance trade networks are reshaping civilisations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and the Steppe (limited contemporary documentation)

events.title: Sea Peoples Invasions of Egypt (Ramesses III's Second Year)
events.description: Ramesses III faces invasions by Sea Peoples groups in the second year of his reign. Egyptian sources (Medinet Habu reliefs and Papyrus Harris I) describe attacks on the Delta and coastal regions. The invasions are part of a sustained pattern of pressure that has been building since c. 1200 BCE. Egypt mobilises military resources to defend the Delta. These invasions represent a major threat to Egyptian stability and mark the beginning of Egypt's decline as a major Mediterranean power. The exact composition and origins of the Sea Peoples remain debated.

sources.name: Medinet Habu temple reliefs and inscriptions

events.certainty_note: Contemporary Egyptian monumental evidence. The second regnal year of Ramesses III is well-established in Egyptological chronology.
cross_references: Part of broader Sea Peoples invasions (c. -1200 to -1150)

events.title: Hittite Empire: Final Collapse and Abandonment of Hattusa
events.description: The Hittite Empire undergoes final collapse. The capital city of Hattusa is abandoned c. 1180 BCE (dating is approximate to within ±5 years). The collapse is marked by destruction, fire, and abandonment of the palace and administrative centres. The causes of the collapse are debated: invasion by Sea Peoples, internal civil war, climate disruption, and economic breakdown have all been proposed. Archaeological evidence from Hattusa shows destruction layers and abandonment. The collapse of the Hittite Empire removes a major stabilising force in the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East, contributing to the broader systemic crisis of the Late Bronze Age collapse.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Hattusa (excavation, stratigraphy, destruction layers)

events.certainty_note: Archaeological evidence from Hattusa confirms destruction and abandonment c. 1180 BCE. Dating is approximate to within ±5 years based on pottery typology and radiocarbon dating.
cross_references: Leads to fragmentation of Hittite successor states (c. -1177 onwards)

events.title: Mycenaean Greece: Continued Collapse and Migration
events.description: Mycenaean palatial centres continue to be abandoned or depopulated. Archaeological evidence from multiple sites shows declining occupation and loss of palatial administration. Population movements are occurring, with some groups migrating to Cyprus, the Levant, and possibly Egypt (as part of the Sea Peoples). The transition from Bronze Age to Iron Age is underway. Linear B script is no longer in use. This marks the deepening of the Greek Dark Ages. The palace at Pylos was destroyed c. 1180 BCE; other major centres are in decline.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Mycenaean sites (Pylos, Mycenae, Tiryns, Athens)
Historiographic consensus on Late Bronze Age collapse

events.certainty_note: Stratigraphic and pottery evidence from multiple sites confirms depopulation and abandonment in this period. Pottery typology and radiocarbon dating support dating to c. 1180–1150 BCE.
cross_references: Part of broader Late Bronze Age collapse (c. -1200 to -1150)

events.title: Shang Dynasty: Final Years
events.description: The Shang dynasty continues in its final phase. King Zhou remains in power but is increasingly isolated and despotic. Zhou pressure from the west is mounting. Oracle bone inscriptions from this period are sparse, suggesting administrative breakdown. The transition to Zhou rule will occur within the next few years. This year falls within the final decade of Shang rule, a period of political instability and military pressure.
events.certainty_note: Dating is approximate based on traditional chronology and later chronicles. Modern chronology places the Shang-Zhou transition at c. 1050–1045 BCE, but this is debated.

disconfirming_evidence: The Hittite collapse is sometimes attributed to invasion by Sea Peoples, but evidence for this is limited. Internal factors (civil war, economic disruption, climate change) may have been more significant. The dating of the Hittite collapse is approximate to within ±5 years; some scholars place it slightly earlier (c. 1185 BCE) or later (c. 1175 BCE). The 'Late Bronze Age collapse' is sometimes presented as a sudden, catastrophic event, but archaeological evidence suggests a more gradual process of decline over several decades. The Sea Peoples invasions may be exaggerated in Egyptian sources.
historiographic_note: Egyptian sources dominate the historical record for this year. Hittite and Mycenaean history is known primarily through archaeology. Chinese history relies on later compilations and oracle bones. The 'Late Bronze Age collapse' is a modern historiographic framework; ancient sources do not describe events in these terms. Egyptian sources are propagandistic and may overstate military successes. The causes of the Late Bronze Age collapse remain debated: climate change, volcanic activity, migration, warfare, and economic disruption have all been proposed. The Hittite collapse is one of the most significant events of this period, but its causes and exact dating remain uncertain.

graph_edges.note: Hittite collapse leads to fragmentation of successor states
Sea Peoples invasions continue and lead to Battle of the Delta three years later
Mycenaean collapse is part of broader Greek Dark Ages transition
